focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 8

By ten-thirty the line had curled past the detergent wall and around the dead change machine, and Jo had torn the pink ticket roll off the hook she usually used for broken dryers because Blue Hour had run out of respectable ways to pretend it was anything except what it had become.

Mira stood behind a folding card table with a marker in one hand and a cup of coffee gone gray and bitter in the other and tried to sort urgency from shame before either one turned into a fight. Jo had taped butcher paper to the front of the table and written categories in thick black strokes.

`BLEEDING`

`PANIC`

`MOTION`

`MEMORY`

`I DON'T KNOW`

The last column was already longest.

"If you only came to wash your clothes," Jo said to the room at large, "I need you to decide whether your problem is laundry or God. I can only fix one of those in here."

That got a few thin laughs and one offended sniff from a woman hugging a mesh bag of uniforms to her chest. It also kept the line from folding in on itself, which was what Jo actually cared about. She stood in practical black shoes planted wide on the tile, silver braid down her back, a pencil jammed through it like a skewer, and snapped tickets off the roll with the authority of somebody distributing medicine after a storm.

Mira took ticket twelve from a man who smelled of freezer brine and old aftershave. Noel Lima had a split thumbnail wrapped in athletic tape and a silver tooth that flashed every time the laundromat radio caught an organ chord. He flinched so hard his folding chair scraped backward.

"Not pain?" Mira asked.

"Not exactly." He pressed both fists against his thighs. The borrowed forearms twitched, wanting to rise and guard his face. "Every time I hear church music this body thinks somebody's about to ring a...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...d maybe choir practice. Mira almost thanked him for the joke. Then Yaret stepped through the side door from the administrative corridor and the lobby shrank around the sight of him.

He was alone now, coat over one arm, expression unchanged from the conference room. He saw Mira at the table, saw the open bag, saw the guard's hands in it. If he found any satisfaction in the scene, he kept it private.

"Ms. Alves," he said. "We may need another conversation tomorrow."

"I'm touched."

"Do not destroy anything tonight."

"You already made me sign the part where I don't."

"Signatures help some people remember."

He said it mildly. He could have been discussing weatherproofing.

The guard zipped Mira's bag and returned it. "All set."

Mira slung it over her shoulder. The wafer bit when she shifted her weight. She did not limp. Years of rent had trained her in more important disguises than that.

Outside, the canal wind came hard around the annex corners and shoved filtered office air out of her lungs. Through the glass lobby wall she could still see Yaret speaking to the guard, Celia crossing the corridor beyond with a file tucked to her side, everyone arranged inside the building's white light like figures in a refrigerated case. Tomorrow at eight she was meant to walk back into the badge and the desk and the questions, with the job continuing long enough to kill whatever it touched.

She started uphill toward Brine Court with the stolen wafer hidden in her shoe and the inquiry notice in her bag, one hard proof and one official threat. Every step reminded her they were both real.

By the time she reached the tram stop she knew something simple and expensive: CommonFrame had stopped being a workplace she could use. It was evidence storage with her name already on the shelf.
